title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
I perform a lot of one-shot analysis and need to know it’s right.
logo pinched from r-bloggers.com, great site, you should check it out

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
What do you actually test?
The code ends up being either a few complicated scripts or a simple and dedicated program, dependent on which way round you want to view something very single-purpose that takes specifically formatted csv files as inputs :P A person can end up doing a lot of weird maths in a small number of lines, and the information is often being used to make important decisions in a hurry, so those numbers need to be ironclad. There is guidance available on what a QA process should look like at a high level; who should be involved, how to record it and what sign-off looks like, in the famous (kind of, in Gov circles, don’t judge me…) Aqua Book.
My issue with this is it’s high level and software agnostic; it doesn’t demonstrate what to check or how, and I need to somehow turn that into actual code that passes or fails my crap. This is a shame when analysts are more and more moving towards R rather than Excel, where writing quality/data tests almost in the style of unit tests or end-to-end tests should be possible, and is an obvious safeguard for any analysis that may one day require replication.
Not a new problem though! Others got to this long before me. This post records some methods and guides I’ve turfed up on what to actually do in building something.

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
First, properly from the start
A far more comprehensive guide for analysts to writing and structuring reproducible, QA’able code, including information on how to peer review code, is available in the Duck Book, created by the Office for National Statistics. Includes helpful guides to matters like setting up YAML files for readable experiment configuration files.



Duck Book logo, extremely cool


Another interesting looking book is the Researcher Programming Resources - From the University of Sheffield, covering best practices in R, Python and Matlab (“now, that’s a name I’ve not heard in a long time…”), including a nice pile of additional book recommendations at least for R on a variety of statistical and simulation topics. You can never have enough badly named PDF files in your reference folder.

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
section: On setup: use tidyverse in the first place
text:
On setup: use tidyverse in the first place
Tidyverse is way more readable, and favours explicit naming of columns/variables when manipulating, which lends itself to readable code and makes human error in referencing the wrong stuff far less likely. AI exists I hear - if you want to do something and can’t think of a tidyverse way of doing it, ask one ;)
df <- iris %>%
  tibble() %>%
  filter(Species == "setosa") %>%
  select(Petal.Length, Petal.Width, Species)

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
Input: data quality
Check what you think you’re putting in. Manually checking (boo) some aspects of the input data against their external source if possible can be good (for example, to check the right filters were applied in downloading from a portal of some kind). If those things can be checked by scanning the data itself for min/max values eg; of year, or counts of number of countries included, even better. Automate that shit and go live your life.
With thanks to Matt Dancho, who wrote Assess your Data Quality in R so no one else has to. It mentions Skimr, an R package that offers a function skim() that provides an enhanced summary of a dataset over the base summary() function.
Using summary() or skim() to eyeball your input data is a great start, check that ranges (min/max) and formats are as expected, and that any counts of NA are also broadly expected. Since 99.99 % of the time you’re ingesting a weird CSV file, double-check that you’ve got the columns you expect and that no extras or weird broken fields have been created through poorly formatted text.
If you have some reason for checking a specific column, as well as using the above functions to check for NA values and for sensible min/max values, eyeballing the most common values can be useful to check for weird/repeating/dominating values:

df %>% pull(<column>) %>% table() (for counts of values)
df %>% pull(<column>) %>% unique() (to see all unique values)


For repeats: automate those checks with validate
Thank you statology.org for this guide, How to Automate Data Quality Checks with R by Jayita Gulati.
The library offers some very simple syntax for defining “validators” that check a dataframe conforms to user-defined rules on what values should be in which columns. Easier to imagine using this for input validation but there’s no reason one couldn’t instead use it to validate outputs are formatted as expected and within a certain range if you have a program you may run again. The report it creates also helpfully reports NA values, which is again a pretty handy thing to check on work output, where weird maths issues often manifest as NA.
library(validate)

# Define data quality rules
rules <- validator(
  Petal.Length >= 1.0 & Petal.Length <= 1.7,                 
  Species %in% c("setosa", "virginica")                       
)

# Apply the rules to the dataset
results <- confront(iris, rules)

# Summarize the results
summary_results <- summary(results)
print(summary_results)
Output:
  name items passes fails nNA error warning
1   V1   150     48   102   0 FALSE   FALSE
2   V2   150    100    50   0 FALSE   FALSE
                                                expression
1 Petal.Length - 1 >= -1e-08 & Petal.Length - 1.7 <= 1e-08
2                   Species %vin% c("setosa", "virginica")
The example above adapted from How to Automate Data Quality Checks with R by Jayita Gulati.
In base R can use if and stop to terminate execution or print out a message if some test is not met. I’ve used it crudely to simplify and encapsulate tests in scripts.
(I’ve also cheated and broken out of base R to use glue to compose a reasonably informative message…)
library(glue)

if (sum(df$sectors) != total) {
    stop(
        glue("Sum of sectoral values is greater than total.  Sum: {sum(df$sectors)}, Total: {total}")
    )
}
There’s also a library called assertthat, which makes the tests a little clearer in return for an extra dependency to track.
library(glue)
library(assertthat)

assert_that(
    sum(df$sectors) <= total,
    msg = glue("Sum of sectoral values is greater than total.  Sum: {sum(df$sectors)}, Total: {total}")
)

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
Functions: maths is correct
You used R and not Excel, so you’re ahead of the game. As a reminder of the problems you now don’t have, the maths is applied to the whole dataframe by default, no hunting for that one cell the formula somehow missed. The equations directly reference variables/columns by name, no searching for incorrect cell references. That’s about 99 % of human Excel errors cut out by default (percentage; total guess…).
Some tips for transparency in your maths: - Universal values/parameters should either be in a configuration file, or defined clearly at the top of the script. Consider following the python convention of using ALL_CAPS for parameters that are global to your program. - Complex calculations can be broken up into multiple operations (even within a single “mutate” call) to make the purpose of each part of the calculation apparent and to make execution order of the calculations completely clear. - You can add # Comments to the end of every line, DO IT.
An additional advantage of breaking up complex calculations into multiple stages with intermediate values stored in columns so that those intermediate values can be validated as above, using validator or assert or any other trick. Unfortunately, exactly what checks might make sense for some value is the context dependent part you’ll have to invent yourself.

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
Actually: lets address configuration explicitly
Because it really helps with transparency. At minimum, configuration information for your experiment or process should be at the top of the script and clearly marked as the configuration space, but that’s minimum, not recommended practice. A better option (for R) is to store your configuration including filepaths in a separate R file (perhaps config.R) that you then load at the start of your program with source(), so a file like this:
config.R
input_path <- "path/to/input.csv"
output_path <- "path/to/results.csv"

years_to_process <- c(2022, 2023, 2024)

elasticity <- 4.0
An even better option is an entirely separate configuration file in some format specifically intended for it, like YAML. I’m not going to go into that here because I don’t yet have anything complicated enough to need it but you can find an excellent guide on all this in the Duck Book (configuration chapter).

title: Quality Assurance of Analysis in R
text:
Output: validate and sense-check
The same methods used to validate input data can be used to validate output data. The final step of figuring out what numbers would make sense is the pain in the ass bit requiring real thought unfortunately. I suggest creating a space in your code for tests and a few rough tests early - that way you can add tests as you think of them through the project. Every time you sense check something for yourself in building the code, you’ve just executed something that might be a useful test to keep in and run everytime the program runs. Let them accumulate.
Consider also sense-checking your numbers against external sources. A rough example (not tested); if you are calculating trade figures for America, do a bit of googling and find out if your numbers make sense given total trade with America, which should be an easy number to find. If your calculation of value of back massagers sold in the USA out-values all trade for the last three years, you make have missplaced a zero.

title: Save csv file from Pandas that Excel won’t ruin
text:
Saving files that Excel won’t ruin
Deal with a lot of columns that look like integers but are actually ID columns that can have leading zeroes.
I also deal with a lot of human text with special characters, when saved in a csv file in UTF-8 as normal, Excel often fails to detect and correctly render the UTF-8 formatting due to being generally terrible software.
How to save a file (from Python) that excel will open, interpret and display correctly with these problems:
import csv
import pandas as pd

df = ...

# Excel will interpret cells that start with a '-' as formulas
df['some_field'] = df['some_field'].str.replace("- ", "")

df.to_csv(
"data/output/saved_file.csv", 
quoting=csv.QUOTE_NONNUMERIC,                   # Quote all non-numeric fields in the output file, which forces excel to read as string
encoding="utf-8-sig",                           # An encoding that adds a UTF-8 BOM (Byte Order Mark) that tells excel the doc is in UTF-8
index=False                                     # Don't add a column for the index, only need this if your index is meaningless
)

title: Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
text:
Tools
The Unix philosophy (Art of Unix Programming is a great text). Then pipe between those (good, focussed) tools. Based on management of stdin, stdout, stderr. If you really need an example of this, type rev and play around.
A very short example, counting the number of chapters in Adventures in Wonderland (including downloading with curl)
$ curl -s "https://www.gutenberg.org/files/11/11-0.txt" |
> grep " CHAPTER" |
> wc -l
12
(And use > and >> to output to a file if you want, or read from those files with cat).
2> redirects the error messages to files, used this in Data Engineering at least once while debugging something long-running in an inadvisably hands-on manner in Azure.
Lots of the tools used are actually non-standard bits created by Jeronen or others - though that these can be created, distributed and reused so easily is kind of the point. Jeroen’s own tools - dsutls

Handy bit for installing the tools I’ve been playing with:
sudo apt install cowsay rust-bat moreutils csvkit
Some things I’ve found super useful: csvlook/csv2md, batcat (in book as “bat” but there’s a name clash when I install on 24.04), tee for pushing out intermediate results. And of course, I always forget “man” is an option because we have the internet now… and it turns out I can press “/” to search these pages! Probably other things that open in this reader mode! jq super useful when dealing with web data/API’s/config files. Literally never heard of the tldr command…
csvlook -I prevents type inference and formatting eg; of numbers (interpreted years as numbers and added thousand commas without this).

csvgrep for searching/filtering csv
jq for working through json keys

$ < wikimedia-stream-sample sed -n 's/^data: //p' |
> jq 'select(.type == "edit" and .server_name == "en.wikipedia.org") | .title'

title: Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
text:
Making scripts
For example, here’s a surprisingly sophisticated bash script:
See this page
curl -sL "https://www.gutenberg.org/files/11/11-0.txt" |
> tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' |
> grep -oE "[a-z\']{2,}" |
> sort |
> uniq -c |
> sort -nr |
> head -n 10
Just start writing your commands, if no input is defined in your script, will take from stdin, which means you can pipe stuff at it. Defining arguments is simple too. Names them rather than using directly for readability.
See example here

title: Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
text:
Documentation and security
It mentions docopt as a tool for adding help docs? Should look in to that. Also ShellCheck for scanning bash code for issues/vulnerabilities.

title: Book Notes: Data Science at the Command Line
text:
Thoughts

Book is mostly useful for learning bash/shell/zsh/unix if you don’t know it already, that’s me for sure, probably not essential. Also, the command line is absolutely the kind of thing that’s so well documented that AI will be brilliant at it but who cares about that.
I like that he’s fairly casual about not explaining every flag, command and use in the book, you need to go look things up if you want details and that’s always a good learning mechanism.
There are definitely tools in here I’m going to abuse - curl, weirdly, I just find more elegant than messing with python requests, and coupled with jq and csv type tools I can write something that’ll just work in a lot of places (read: VM’s in Azure) without having to mess around with venv. I also love being able to turn csv into markdown tables though I bet that exists inside Python!
I like that he uses Nano, I know clever editors based on keyboards and nothing but keyboards exist, and I will never have time to care. Actual typing is not the least efficient part of my day.
I notice also that for applications for which tools already exist, the python version is more verbose (albeit possibly more readable? I’m not confident on that)
The continuous allowance for streaming data is really interesting also, not something I’ve ever had to consider as a “research” data scientist

title: Making a Gantt Chart in Python
text:
Code for generating Gantt Charts, pretty ones, in Plotly.
Read the original Plotly examples if you want to get started marginally quicker, and to see more examples of variants of these plots. Mine’s got some extra plotly arguments and a marginally more complete pile of code at the end.
Uses Python, Pandas and Plotly. The project has two logical components; creating a suitable dataframe representation of the tasks to be completed, and plotting that. Simple! Credit to this Plotly guide for providing all the code I’m going to adapt. The only real change I need is the ability to visualise dependencies between tasks.
My repo for this



Example Gantt chart visualisation

title: Making a Gantt Chart in Python
text:
Code for generating Gantt Charts, pretty ones, in Plotly.
Read the original Plotly examples if you want to get started marginally quicker, and to see more examples of variants of these plots. Mine’s got some extra plotly arguments and a marginally more complete pile of code at the end.
Uses Python, Pandas and Plotly. The project has two logical components; creating a suitable dataframe representation of the tasks to be completed, and plotting that. Simple! Credit to this Plotly guide for providing all the code I’m going to adapt. The only real change I need is the ability to visualise dependencies between tasks.
My repo for this



Example Gantt chart visualisation

title: Making a Gantt Chart in Python
text:
Why?
Gantt charts are boring and I dislike excel intensely so I’m going to build this instead. This is probably not the best approach. The best approach would be something that integrates with your DevOps platform, but I don’t know any and am not currently working with one ;)
A second best approach would be dedicated software, there seem to be a lot of free Gantt chart generators available. I’ve used the ugly but standalone (and free) GanttProject before, which nicely handles dependencies between tasks and produces functional output. It’s a good quick option.
But if I build it in Python I will have a tool I can easily point at different underlying data for each project I need one for. Plus Plotly gives me scalable html output with mouseover text, which is pretty handy when there’s a lot of optional context to tasks.

title: Making a Gantt Chart in Python
text:
Creating the data
Script expects a csv file with columns to describe the tasks and their start and end dates. I’ve added one for dependencies, but it’s just a text column that gets displayed really.



Task
Start
Finish
Dependencies
Resource




“Job A”
‘2025-06-01’
‘2025-08-20’

Bill


“Job B”
‘2025-08-20’
‘2025-11-28’
‘Job A’
Ben


“Job C”
‘2025-09-28’
‘2025-11-28’
‘Job A’
Bill


Job D”
‘2025-12-01’
‘2026-01-28’
‘Job B’
Bill


“Job E”
‘2025-12-01’
‘2026-01-28’
‘Job C’
Ben

title: Making a Gantt Chart in Python
section: Plotting using plotly express’s timeline method
text:
Plotting using plotly express’s timeline method
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd


# Load the data (see section above)
df = pd.read_csv("example.csv", dtype=str)

# Create the labelling for the task blocks (I want to see task dependencies)
df['Labelling'] = np.where(
    df['Dependencies'].isnull(),
    df['Task'],
    df['Task'] + ", Dependencies: " + df["Dependencies"]
)

# Order as I want plotted
df = df.sort_values("Start", ascending=False)

# Plot
fig = px.timeline(
    df,
    x_start="Start",
    x_end="Finish",
    y="Task",
    color="Resource",
    text="Labelling",
    hover_name="Resource",
    hover_data={"Resource": False, "Start": True, "Finish": True, "Labelling": False},
    title="Gantt Chart"
)

fig.show()

title: About Martin
text:
Martin is a “technologist” currently working in the public sector as a data scientist, with an interest in all things tech, AI and cyberpunk! His office time is spent applying NLP and LLM tools in the field of information retrieval, getting piles of government records and administrative data to talk back to him. There’s a side of dashboarding (RShiny), Pythonic web development (Flask) and the occasional foray into the Azure ecosystem. Currently there’s a lot of data processing and statistics (R).
There are also the weird project requests, building tools to figure out where the buses are, or what just happened to the jobs market.
His free time is spent playing co-op PC games, reading bad sci-fi, hiking and camping.

title: Crib sheet: handling python environments through different tools
text:
Quick cribsheets for handling python environments through different tools
Cover art courtesy Google’s Nano Banana

title: Crib sheet: handling python environments through different tools
text:
Pip
python3 -m venv .env                        # Create venv off whatever python version you call from in folder .env
source .env/bin/activate
python -m pip install --update pip          # Bring pip up-to-date first
pip install -r requirements.txt             # install modules listed in requirements.txt
Pip documentation - requirements file format

title: Crib sheet: handling python environments through different tools
text:
Conda
Creating it first time:
conda create --name environment_name
conda activate environment_name
conda install numpy pandas pdfplumber ipykernel jupyter openpyxl  # installing like this out of laziness
conda env export > environment.yml                                # writing an environment file so I can properly restore it
To build the environment using the stored environment.yml:
conda env create -f environment.yml                     # recreate the environment described in that environment
conda activate environment_name                         # activate

title: Crib sheet: handling python environments through different tools
text:
Bonus: renv (R)
install.packages("renv")           # Install environment manager
renv::init()                       # First run, create environment for project (current dir)
renv::activate()                   # Or activate after navigating to project
install.packages("readr")          # Install packages
renv::snapshot()                   # Store exact package configuration to renv.lock file
renv::restore()                    # Recreate environment from renv.lock file
renv::revert()                     # Revert environment to that specified in renv.lock file

title: Book Notes: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
text:
Cover art for book, Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs


Oh dear lord the maths.


My current data science office is pretty driven, conveniently one of our number is a very experienced programmer and has offered to help guide us through reading Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs (a helpful PDF version there). This book is reportedly a bit special, with a reputation for turning people’s brains around and change their view of programming. It’s also pretty old, dating from back when people really had to care about what they could fit into memory and how performant their algorithms were (as opposed to the Data Science approach of “Too slow eh? More cores/memory/VM’s/funding!”). The foreword begins:

This is the second edition SICP book, from Unofficial Texinfo Format. You are probably reading it in an Info hypertext browser, such as the Info mode of Emacs. You might alternatively be reading it TEX-formatted on your screen or printer, though that would be silly.

In hindsight this perfectly captured what I was in for - created by people who use Emacs and think formatted text is a sign of weakness…
I’m one of the many people who are now kind of reversing into computer science, having used computational tools for data analysis before steadily sinking into more sophisticated programming tools as my problems became more complicated and my need to perform shadow IT around organisational limitations became more and more pressing. I suspect it’s a natural consequence of all modern companies accidentally becoming IT companies, whether they want to or not.



Short version: While the actual text is very readable, the exercises were very much meant for people with a stronger background in maths. I studied a natural science, integration was considered a bit fancy. A lot of the (probably elegant) exercise questions in this book involve implementing some equation that would probably make perfect sense to me and be obviously relevant if I hadn’t spent my degree dripping acid on rocks to see if they fizzed.
As it is I was left staring at the question wondering if I’m supposed to care or if a “trigonometric identity” might just mean a triangle or something? Whatever. Thankfully our office’s tame wizard is well aware, and is able to help direct us to what we need to know and are meant to be drawing from sections and exercises. It’s also abundantly clear that this is a course textbook, meant to be drawn from by a teacher as needed. Knowing this is useful both in that it reassures me it’s ok to seek help and that I can occasionally skip an impenetrably mathematical exercise and lose nothing but knowledge of some weird and unusual number series. I’m ok with that.
The book is very much still worth working through - it leaves me knowing some more of the terminology and concepts I feel I’ve missed in my backwards slide into working with computers. A goal of mine is to steadily work through the curriculum set out by Teach Yourself Computer Science, which suggests getting through chapters 1 - 3 including exercises.



Having only just reached the end of Chapter 1, I’ve picked up lots of basics, but in more clearly defined detail than I’ve previously owned them. For example, the book has gone into detail on recursive vs iterative algorithms, and how to map from one to the other, and how they are executed by the interpreter. Terminology I didn’t know I needed or thought I understood from general knowledge has been clarified as something more specific in CS (Computer Science), like the difference between a recursive algorithm and a recursive process.
The insight into what interpreters think of/about the code I write, and the numerous illustrations of how execution of a more complex program folds out into a tree-like structure of executed primitive operations, are both giving me a view of how computers work that I may have seen before but is now far more explicit, and stands in attractive contrast to the many hands-on high level tutorials on applications of programming to things like data analysis that pay little attention to what is going on below the level of Pandas’ methods.
At a higher level, there’s also been some good discussion and examples in the book of proper abstraction; building procedures to perform specific, higher level tasks that are clear and focussed enough that once defined, you can use those without ever needing to reconsider their internal workings. Abstraction and modular code are ideas that’ll be old-hat to most people, but the
And of course, now I know some Scheme! I have to admit its simplicity and lack of too much syntactic sugar (see, I know bigger words now!) make it a very nice language for demonstrating how the interpreter works through the instructions you’re feeding it. And being forced to be conscious of all those nested brackets assists your awareness of execution order.



I’m using Racket, a descendant of scheme that I chose purely because I could easily download and install it on my Windows laptop, so that I could learn offline on trains (welcome to the North of England, we don’t trust in pervasive internet up here). I’ve been using that in conjunction with the PDF version of the book from MIT, because I’m old so I like that it looks like a textbook that way.
I know others working through the book have been using online implementations of Scheme for simplicity and because our work laptops won’t trust us to install things, here’s one if you need it: try.scheme.org.
For myself, so far I’ve been copy-pasting code snippets over from the book and my notes like a pleb. In all of chapter 1, only once was I working on a compound procedure (big words again! Read: a small program with multiple functions) where I felt like using an IDE properly might help :P



It’s rare that my work either as a data scientist or as a data engineer has required CS knowledge - but on occassion, every few months or so, I find myself having to write something that is effectively performing a tree search to dig through weirdly stored logs. Or I need to perform matrix multiplication to create a speedier version of some geographic operation that geopandas takes hours on. On these weird issues I need to do actual programming and this book is good instruction and practice fodder for that.
I know I’ve got a lot to learn on how to actually structure a project, at every level. A better understanding of how to abstract code, and when to do so, will be useful as I move to a few larger projects. At a fine level, learning more about the proper use of lexical scope and computational complexity will let me write clearer, simpler code that won’t hit “that won’t scale” gotcha’s.

title: Book Notes: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
text:
Having only just reached the end of Chapter 1, I’ve picked up lots of basics, but in more clearly defined detail than I’ve previously owned them. For example, the book has gone into detail on recursive vs iterative algorithms, and how to map from one to the other, and how they are executed by the interpreter. Terminology I didn’t know I needed or thought I understood from general knowledge has been clarified as something more specific in CS (Computer Science), like the difference between a recursive algorithm and a recursive process.
The insight into what interpreters think of/about the code I write, and the numerous illustrations of how execution of a more complex program folds out into a tree-like structure of executed primitive operations, are both giving me a view of how computers work that I may have seen before but is now far more explicit, and stands in attractive contrast to the many hands-on high level tutorials on applications of programming to things like data analysis that pay little attention to what is going on below the level of Pandas’ methods.
At a higher level, there’s also been some good discussion and examples in the book of proper abstraction; building procedures to perform specific, higher level tasks that are clear and focussed enough that once defined, you can use those without ever needing to reconsider their internal workings. Abstraction and modular code are ideas that’ll be old-hat to most people, but the
And of course, now I know some Scheme! I have to admit its simplicity and lack of too much syntactic sugar (see, I know bigger words now!) make it a very nice language for demonstrating how the interpreter works through the instructions you’re feeding it. And being forced to be conscious of all those nested brackets assists your awareness of execution order.

title: Book Notes: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
text:
I’m using Racket, a descendant of scheme that I chose purely because I could easily download and install it on my Windows laptop, so that I could learn offline on trains (welcome to the North of England, we don’t trust in pervasive internet up here). I’ve been using that in conjunction with the PDF version of the book from MIT, because I’m old so I like that it looks like a textbook that way.
I know others working through the book have been using online implementations of Scheme for simplicity and because our work laptops won’t trust us to install things, here’s one if you need it: try.scheme.org.
For myself, so far I’ve been copy-pasting code snippets over from the book and my notes like a pleb. In all of chapter 1, only once was I working on a compound procedure (big words again! Read: a small program with multiple functions) where I felt like using an IDE properly might help :P

title: Book Notes: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
text:
It’s rare that my work either as a data scientist or as a data engineer has required CS knowledge - but on occassion, every few months or so, I find myself having to write something that is effectively performing a tree search to dig through weirdly stored logs. Or I need to perform matrix multiplication to create a speedier version of some geographic operation that geopandas takes hours on. On these weird issues I need to do actual programming and this book is good instruction and practice fodder for that.
I know I’ve got a lot to learn on how to actually structure a project, at every level. A better understanding of how to abstract code, and when to do so, will be useful as I move to a few larger projects. At a fine level, learning more about the proper use of lexical scope and computational complexity will let me write clearer, simpler code that won’t hit “that won’t scale” gotcha’s.

title: Jellyfin server - from DVD ripping to watching films in the bath
text:
I want to get a Jellyfin server up and running with the rips of my (legally owned) DVD’s. Yes, I still have lots of DVD’s. I’ve even acquired more, mostly terrible B movies, because I don’t need HD to drink beer and watch a badly animated shark eating even less animated actors.
There’s a few steps in this journey and I want to keep them in case I ever need to do this again. More specifically I never want to have to go learn about Linux user group permissions a second time.
Blu-ray isn’t covered here because that little encryption war is insane, as is the cost of a player that stands a chance of actually playing your disk through the haze of weird proprietary formats and bloat.
Gemini was used to figure out linux file permissions and drive mounting, and also to remind myself of the slightly laborious settings maze I had to navigate to get all the audio and subtitle tracks out of a disk in the MKV format.
If like me you found the numerical codes used for permissions fairly confusing, here’s a good guide for later, on how they work under linux courtesy DigitalOcean.


Jellyfin has a simple client/server setup; I set up one computer on my network to serve media and any other computer on my home network (or android device like my tablet or TV) can connect to that server. I’m not fussed about making the media available remotely, I have GB’s of storage on every portable device for taking stuff with me and then I’m not reliant on wifi or signal (I hike, cycle and camp, I don’t expect connectivity in my life :P )



Handbrake. It’s free, but not so easy, because there are a crap-ton of options. It’s a technical tool for technical people. Two options from opposite ends of the techie spectrum;


This should be able to play on any device anywhere without complaining, and the file size is not bad, usually < 1GB for a film (sometimes a bit more).

After selecting the source (the DVD in the presumed DVD drive) select “Very Fast 1080p30” to start with.
In the Summary tab select MP4 format if it isn’t selected already.
In the Dimensions tab disable upscaling to keep the native resolution The software you play films through later will handle any upscaling, so you gain nothing by doing that work in advance except to create a larger file.
In the Video tab select “Constant Framerate” on the left. Some players have trouble with varying framerates so this maximises compatibility.
Still in the Video tab leave the “Quality” slider on its default value. This slider trades of video quality against video compression, the default (20, I think) is well chosen :)
Finally, use the Subtitles tab to pick one Subtitles track to burn into the video if you want it. Beware it’ll be burned in, meaning part of the video, not a digital overlay you can switch off. If you want selectable subtitles you’ll need the other option…




Matroska Video Files can hold multiple video, audio and subtitle tracks. Pick this for keeping all your subtitle tracks and alternative audio tracks from the DVD. In return the video file will be a couple of GB, and the MKV container format isn’t supported so universally. Jellyfin does support it, so it’s a good option if building that solution is your only goal.

After selecting the source as above, select the “MKV” format in the “Summary” tab
You still need to select all the video encoding settings because MKV is a container for other tracks, not the actual encoding. The rest of the instructions are those for the MP4 above.
In the “Audio” tab you’ll need to set it to detect ANY language track, by clicking “Selection Behaviour” and under “chosen languages” moving the “any” option into the right-hand column. Then you’ll need to tell it to rip EVERY detected language track by selecting “All matching tracks” under “Track Selection”. An unintended consequence: if there’s a directors commentary you’ll be getting that too. Sorry.
In the “Subtitles” tab you do similar, use “Selection Behaviour” and under “Chosen Languages” move “any” to the right. Clear the “burn-in” checkboxes (this option would scar subtitles directly onto the video file, rather than them being separate files overlayed, which means you could never switch them off). Check “Allow SRT/SSA/ASS passthrough” and “Allow DVD/BD/Text passthrough”, which will save them as files within the Matroska container.








Here’s their documentation pages. I have a fairly ancient x86 laptop that’s going to do the honours.
Short version for linux people:
# Run their script from their website
curl https://repo.jellyfin.org/install-debuntu.sh | sudo bash
You can use the URL to go look at that script, or you can just run it on your entirely disposable spare ancient laptop ;)
For any other linux distros they have docker container images. Windows and MacOS installation is simply an executable installer and a DMG file respectively.



Jellyfin gets its own user called jellyfin and its own group, also called jellyfin, when installed on Linux. This complicates file access because it doesn’t have access to your user account’s files by default. At this point I remembered “oh yeah, built by linux users” and gently slapped myself in the face for thinking I’d be done running just one script. This complication is why I suddenly needed to learn about Linux user groups.


If whatever antique computer you’re victimising for this has enough storage already, you’re pretty much there. Easiest is to create a folder in a shared location on the drive (suggest mnt/media) and drop all your files in there. No further steps needed.
Alternatively grant the jellyfin group access to the folder with your media in your home directory or wherever you stashed it. Best way to do that is to create a new group;
sudo groupadd media-users  # New group created
Then add both your own user and jellyfin to the group:
sudo usermod -aG media-users your_user_name  # Add user to group
sudo usermod -aG media-users jellyfin
Set your user as owner, and the new group as the group owner:
sudo chown -R your_user:media-users /path/to/your/media
Finally, the permissions for the folder need to be altered (recursively) so that both the user and the group have full control/access to files and these permissions are inherited by files and sub-folders inside:
chmod -R 2770  # 2 = recursive, 7 = user, 7 = group, 0 = everyone else
Crucial step - REBOOT  or spend the next hour wondering why Jellyfin still can’t see anything (hypothetically) - need to do this for all these membership alterations to take effect.






I want to plug in a very large USB stick and serve media off that. You can do more complicated things like connecting it up to a separate file server but I have a hundred or so MP4 files, not exactly a collection worthy of the Smithsonian. I’m fine copying my collection from my desktop to a remote drive, because then I can be lazy and update that collection by adding files to the drive.



The drive needs to always mount with correct user/group and drive identifier on rebooting the computer for Jellyfin to not lose track of it, so granting Jellyfin access to a USB drive in Linux is a two-part process: Mounting the drive correctly and setting the Permissions.
Use the /etc/fstab file for mounting, then apply permissions to the Jellyfin user.



For a server application like Jellyfin, you need the drive mounted consistently and with the correct user/group ID on boot.
The UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) is the best way to ensure the system mounts the correct drive every time, regardless of which USB port you plug it into.

Plug in the USB drive.
Use the blkid command to find its UUID.
sudo blkid
Look for your drive in the output and copy the UUID value. It looks like a long string of numbers and letters (e.g., UUID="93033593-60b5-38ef-8086-e434e74192b0").




Create a directory where the drive will be mounted, Use a standard location like /mnt/media.
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/usbmedia



This file tells Linux how to mount filesystems at boot. Always back up this file before editing.

Back up your current fstab:
sudo cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak
Open the file with a text editor (e.g., nano):
sudo nano /etc/fstab
Add a new line at the end using the UUID you found, the mount point you created, the filesystem type, and the mount options.
Example fstab entry for an EXT4 drive:
UUID=YOUR_UUID_HERE /mnt/usbmedia ext4 defaults,nofail 0 0

YOUR_UUID_HERE: Replace with the UUID you copied.
ext4: Replace with your drive’s actual filesystem type (e.g., ext4, ntfs, exfat).
nofail: This is important; it tells the system to boot even if the drive isn’t plugged in.

If the drive is NTFS or exFAT (common for USB drives): These filesystems don’t natively support Linux permissions, so you must specify the user and group on mount:
UUID=YOUR_UUID_HERE /mnt/usbmedia ntfs defaults,nofail,unmask=22,uid=jellyfin,gid=jellyfin 0 0

uid=jellyfin,gid=jellyfin: This mounts the entire drive to be owned by the jellyfin user and group, immediately solving the permission problem.
umask=22: Modifies the drive permissions to remove write permissions for other people/users/groups

Save and close the file (Ctrl+O, Enter, Ctrl+X in nano).
Test the mount without rebooting:
systemctl daemon-reload # Reloads system control files?  REF CHECK
sudo mount -a
If successful, the drive is now mounted and ready for permissions.

I had to go back and reformat as ext4, because I had some MKV files larger than ~ 4GB, the largest file size supported by fat32, which I’d left the drive formatted as by default :(



If you used the ntfs/exfat option above, you can skip this step, as the permissions are set by the mount options.
If you are using EXT4 (or another native Linux filesystem) and didn’t use the uid/gid options in fstab, you need to explicitly grant access.

Change Ownership (The Simplest Method): This gives the jellyfin user and group complete control over the files.
sudo chown -R jellyfin:jellyfin /mnt/usbmedia
Set Permissions: This grants the owner (jellyfin) read, write, and execute permissions (7) and the group/others read and execute (5).
sudo chmod -R 755 /mnt/usbmedia




After the drive is mounted and permissions are set, restart the Jellyfin service to ensure it recognizes the new directory structure.
sudo systemctl restart jellyfin
You can now add /mnt/usbmedia as a new library path within the Jellyfin web interface.




It’s helpful if the IP address of your server doesn’t shift every time you restart the machine. If it does, when restarting the client app on your tablet etc after a shift, you’ll have to reconnect, and remember your password all over again.


Logging in to my home router, I found I could reserve specific IP addresses for specific MAC addresses, with the device network name helpfully alongside the MAC to tell me what I was pinning. The MAC address is an arbitrary-seeming string assigned to a network interface by the manufacturer of that network interface, so it serves as a unique ID. By reserving the IP address like this, you won’t need to change anything unless the router or the network device (machine or a network card speifically) change.
In case you’ve not done it before, you can find your router’s login page for messing with settings by directing your web browser to its IP address, eg; 192.168.0.1. To see what your router’s IP address is you’ll need to check the network settings on whatever device you’re using. Too many different devices, so you’ll have to google that yourself :) If you’ve not logged in to it before, you’ll need the admin login details typically hidden on the sticker on your router (yep, that’s what the other password on there was for).



You can also configure a computer so that rather than being randomly assigned an IP address by DHCP (the protocol that does the random assigning), it insists on getting a specific IP address if available. Only becomes a problem if that address isn’t in fact available. Supposedly it’s possible to find out what range of IP addresses the router uses for DHCP (the last number in the four number address, goes from 0 to 255 max on IPv4). Then you pick an IP address higher than that. My router seemed to let me restrict the range it used manually in its Settings page. Googling this option is left as an exercise for the reader, because there’s lots of devices and I didn’t do it anyway ;)

title: Reminding myself how to use Quarto to build this website
text:
Need these links for setting up.
Download and install, then follow the Github Pages guidance for option 2: making use of quarto’s quarto publish command to push rendered websites up from the command line.
Download Quarto
Quarto (blog) setup guidance
Github pages guidance





quarto preview
Renders the website and serves it up locally so you can see what you just created



quarto render
quarto publish




I tend to just link to images on other people’s websites when discussing their software, figure the extra networking must do their SEO some good and saves me copying copyrighted images.
If I generate an image (2026 is weird) using just Gemini or something, it can’t seem to handle requests for lower res images or to downscale something it’s produced, which is pretty weak.
So here’s how to use imagemagick to squish an image at the command line:
sudo apt install imagemagick
magick excel_rex_gemini.png -resize 1000x -quality 80 excel_rex_gemini_web.webp
The .webp file extension is a newfangled even more compressed format that saves a lot on filesize so apparently just a good idea by default: Google WebP page
Easy to change to .png if suspicious.
